questions.text: Plants which grow on saline soils are
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Halophytes (C)**

Halophytes are plants specially adapted to grow in soils with high salt concentration (e.g., mangroves, Suaeda). They have salt-excreting glands, succulence and vivipary. In DRDO CEPTAM-11 General Science, this is a high-weightage topic under plant adaptations and economic botany because India has a vast coastline (7,500+ km) where DRDO uses halophytes for coastal bio-shields, erosion control, cyclone protection and soil stabilisation near naval bases and forward posts.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• **Xerophytes (A):** Adapted to dry/arid conditions (deep roots, reduced leaves) — not for saline waterlogging.
• **Hydrophytes (B):** Live in water (aerenchyma, reduced cuticle) — cannot tolerate high salt.
• **Succulents (D):** Store water in fleshy tissues (e.g., cactus) — not specifically for salinity.

**Important Exam Concept:** Plant adaptations in extreme environments + defence applications in coastal warfare and environmental engineering (frequently repeated in DRDO papers).

questions.text: Epiphytes are plants which depend on other plants for
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Mechanical support (B)**

Epiphytes (e.g., orchids, mosses) grow on trees for physical support only; they absorb moisture and nutrients from air/rain. DRDO relevance: biomimicry for lightweight camouflage netting, UAV sensor mounting and forest concealment studies in Northeast border areas.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• **Food (A):** Wrong — epiphytes are not parasitic.
• **Shade (C):** Incidental, not the primary dependence.
• **Water (D):** Absorbed from atmosphere, not host.

**Important Exam Concept:** Symbiotic relationships & biomimicry for defence technology (high-weightage in taxonomy & applied biology).

questions.text: The maximum amount of water, which plants need to grow that is absorbed by the following?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Root hairs (D)**

Root hairs (extensions of epidermal cells) massively increase surface area (10–100 times) for passive water absorption by osmosis. DRDO context: critical for soldier hydration strategies in desert operations and hydroponics-based forward-area ration production.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• **Embryonic zone (A):** Meristematic region — no absorption role.
• **Growing point (B):** Apical meristem — growth, not absorption.
• **Zone of elongation (C):** Cell expansion zone — not primary absorption site.

**Important Exam Concept:** Plant physiology & water relations in extreme environments (repeated in DRDO papers).

questions.text: A hydrophyte is
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: An aquatic plant (B)**

Hydrophytes (e.g., Hydrilla, lotus) live in water with aerenchyma, reduced cuticle and air sacs. DRDO applications: water purification systems for naval bases and aquatic camouflage studies.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• **A marine animal (A):** Wrong kingdom.
• **A plant disease (C):** Unrelated.
• **A rootless plant (D):** Some hydrophytes have roots; not the defining feature.

**Important Exam Concept:** Aquatic adaptations & ecosystem management (naval & border ecology).

questions.text: The radiant energy absorbed by the plants results in
questions.solution:
This question is from **photosynthesis (plant physiology)**, a common topic in **DRDO CEPTAM-11 Tier-1 General Science**.

---

# Question

**The radiant energy absorbed by the plants results in:**

A. Photolysis of water
B. Oxidation of chlorophyll
C. Reduction of oxygen
D. Oxidation of carbon dioxide

**Correct Answer: A. Photolysis of water**

---

# Concept Behind the Question

Plants absorb **radiant energy (light energy)** through **chlorophyll** during the **light reaction of photosynthesis**.

This energy is used to split water molecules into:

[
2H_2O \rightarrow 4H^+ + 4e^- + O_2
]

This process is called **photolysis of water**.

Meaning:

• **Photo** → light
• **Lysis** → splitting

So **light energy splits water molecules**.

Products formed:

• **Oxygen (O₂)** → released into atmosphere
• **Hydrogen ions (H⁺)**
• **Electrons**

This occurs in the **thylakoid membrane of chloroplasts**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Photolysis of water ✔ (Correct)

Radiant energy absorbed by chlorophyll is used to **split water molecules**.

Reaction:

[
2H_2O \rightarrow 4H^+ + 4e^- + O_2
]

Importance:

• Provides **electrons** for photosynthesis
• Produces **oxygen** released by plants

This process occurs in **Photosystem II**.

---

## B. Oxidation of chlorophyll ✘ (Incorrect)

Chlorophyll **absorbs light energy**, but it **does not get oxidized as the main result** of radiant energy absorption.

What actually happens:

• Chlorophyll becomes **excited**
• It releases **high-energy electrons**

So the primary result is **electron excitation**, not oxidation of chlorophyll.

---

## C. Reduction of oxygen ✘ (Incorrect)

Reduction means **gain of electrons**.

In photosynthesis:

• **Oxygen is produced**
• Oxygen is **not reduced**

Instead the reaction is:

[
H_2O \rightarrow O_2
]

This represents **oxidation of water**, not reduction of oxygen.

---

## D. Oxidation of carbon dioxide ✘ (Incorrect)

During photosynthesis, **carbon dioxide is reduced**, not oxidized.

Reaction in the **Calvin Cycle**:

[
CO_2 \rightarrow Glucose
]

This means **CO₂ gains electrons (reduction)**.

Therefore oxidation of CO₂ does **not occur**.

---

# Key Exam Concept (Very Important)

During photosynthesis:

| Substance | Process |
|---|---|
| Water | **Oxidized** |
| Carbon dioxide | **Reduced** |

Shortcut to remember:

**Water loses electrons → Oxygen formed**

**CO₂ gains electrons → Glucose formed**

---

# 1-Line Exam Trick

If the question contains **“radiant energy absorbed by plants”**, the answer is usually:

👉 **Photolysis of water**

---

If needed, you can also study **Photosystem I and Photosystem II**, because many **DRDO / SSC competitive exam MCQs** come from these topics.

questions.text: Which of the following is not correctly matched?
questions.solution:
This question tests **basic ecology and nutrition in organisms**, a topic commonly asked in the **General Science section of DRDO CEPTAM-11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Which of the following is not correctly matched?**

A. Autotroph – Producer
B. Heterotroph – Consumer
C. Saprotroph – Decomposer
D. Herbivore – Secondary consumer

**Correct Answer: D. Herbivore – Secondary consumer**

Because **herbivores are primary consumers**, not secondary consumers.

---

# Concept Required for This Question

In an **ecosystem**, organisms are classified based on **how they obtain food**:

| Category | Role in Ecosystem |
|---|---|
| Producers | Make their own food |
| Consumers | Eat other organisms |
| Decomposers | Break down dead organisms |

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Autotroph – Producer ✔ (Correct match)

**Autotrophs** are organisms that **produce their own food** using:

• **Photosynthesis** (plants, algae)
• **Chemosynthesis** (some bacteria)

Examples:

• Grass
• Trees
• Phytoplankton

They form the **first trophic level** in the food chain.

Example food chain:

[
\text{Grass} \rightarrow \text{Deer} \rightarrow \text{Tiger}
]

Here **grass is the producer**.

Therefore:

**Autotroph = Producer (Correct)**

---

## B. Heterotroph – Consumer ✔ (Correct match)

**Heterotrophs** cannot make their own food.

They obtain energy by **consuming other organisms**.

Examples:

• Humans
• Animals
• Many microorganisms

They are known as **consumers** in ecosystems.

Therefore:

**Heterotroph = Consumer (Correct)**

---

## C. Saprotroph – Decomposer ✔ (Correct match)

**Saprotrophs** obtain nutrition from **dead and decaying organic matter**.

They release **digestive enzymes** that break complex substances into simpler ones.

Examples:

• Fungi
• Certain bacteria

Role in ecosystem:

• **Recycling nutrients**
• **Decomposition of dead organisms**

Thus:

**Saprotroph = Decomposer (Correct)**

---

## D. Herbivore – Secondary consumer ✘ (Incorrect match)

**Herbivores feed directly on plants (producers)**.

Therefore they belong to the **primary consumer level**, not the secondary level.

Example food chain:

[
\text{Grass} \rightarrow \text{Rabbit} \rightarrow \text{Fox}
]

| Organism | Role |
|---|---|
| Grass | Producer |
| Rabbit | **Primary consumer (Herbivore)** |
| Fox | Secondary consumer |

Thus the correct relationship is:

**Herbivore → Primary consumer**

Hence option **D is incorrectly matched**.

---

# Quick Revision for Exams

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Autotroph | Makes own food |
| Producer | Same as autotroph |
| Heterotroph | Depends on others for food |
| Consumer | Eats other organisms |
| Saprotroph | Feeds on dead matter |
| Decomposer | Breaks down dead organisms |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember this **food chain hierarchy**:

[
\text{Producer} \rightarrow \text{Primary Consumer} \rightarrow \text{Secondary Consumer} \rightarrow \text{Tertiary Consumer}
]

Example:

[
\text{Grass} \rightarrow \text{Deer} \rightarrow \text{Tiger}
]

So remember:

• **Herbivore = Primary consumer**
• **Carnivore = Secondary or Tertiary consumer**

---

If needed, you can also practice **important Ecology MCQs commonly asked in DRDO, SSC, and Banking exams**, which frequently help secure **2–3 marks in Tier-1**.

questions.text: Trophic level-I includes
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Green plants (D)**

Producers (autotrophs) form trophic level-I. DRDO relevance: energy flow analysis for sustainable ration supply in remote military camps.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• **Herbivorous animals (A):** Primary consumers (level-II).
• **Carnivorous animals (B):** Secondary/tertiary consumers.
• **Omnivorous animals (C):** Multiple levels.

**Important Exam Concept:** Trophic levels & energy pyramids (ecology for defence sustainability).

questions.text: A group of archaebacteria is used in the production of
questions.solution:
This question comes from **microbiology / classification of organisms**, a topic often asked in the **General Science section of DRDO CEPTAM-11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**A group of archaebacteria is used in the production of**

A. Ethane
B. Methane
C. Acids
D. Alcohols

✅ **Correct Answer: B. Methane**

---

# Core Concept

Some **archaebacteria called methanogens** produce **methane gas (CH₄)** during their metabolic processes.

These microorganisms live in **oxygen-free (anaerobic) environments**, such as:

• Marshy areas
• Sewage treatment plants
• Biogas plants
• Rumen (stomach) of cattle

Because of this property, they are used in **biogas production**, where **organic waste is converted into methane gas**.

Examples of methanogens:

• *Methanobacterium*
• *Methanococcus*

---

# Why Methane is Produced

Methanogens break down organic matter **in the absence of oxygen** and release methane gas.

Reaction:

[
\text{Organic matter} \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2 + \text{other products}
]

This methane gas forms the **main component of biogas**.

Biogas composition (approximate):

| Gas | Percentage |
|---|---|
| Methane | 50–70% |
| Carbon dioxide | 30–40% |
| Other gases | Small amount |

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Ethane ❌ (Incorrect)

Ethane (C₂H₆) is a **hydrocarbon present in natural gas**, but it is **not produced by archaebacteria**.

Methanogens specifically produce **methane**, not ethane.

---

## B. Methane ✅ (Correct)

Methanogenic archaebacteria produce **methane gas (CH₄)**.

This occurs during **anaerobic decomposition of organic matter**.

Applications include:

• **Biogas production**
• **Waste management**
• **Renewable energy generation**

Thus **methane is the correct answer**.

---

## C. Acids ❌ (Incorrect)

Some **bacteria and fungi** produce acids through **fermentation processes** (for example, lactic acid bacteria).

However, **archaebacteria involved in biogas production mainly generate methane**, not acids.

---

## D. Alcohols ❌ (Incorrect)

Alcohol production occurs through **fermentation carried out by yeast**.

Reaction:

[
C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2
]

Organism responsible:

• **Yeast (*Saccharomyces*)**

Archaebacteria are **not involved in alcohol production**.

---

# Important Exam Fact

Among archaebacteria, **methanogens are the most frequently asked group in competitive exams**.

Types of archaebacteria commonly asked:

| Type | Habitat |
|---|---|
| Methanogens | Marshes, rumen of cattle |
| Halophiles | Extremely salty environments |
| Thermoacidophiles | Hot acidic environments |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

If the question mentions:

**“Archaebacteria used in biogas production”**

👉 The answer is **Methane / Methanogens**.

---

If required, you can also study **important questions from “Kingdom Monera and Archaebacteria”**, which frequently appear in **DRDO, SSC, and Banking competitive exams**.

questions.text: How do most insects respire?
questions.solution:
This question tests **respiration in animals (especially insects)**, a common topic in the Biology section of **DRDO CEPTAM-11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**How do most insects respire?**

A. Through skin
B. Through gills
C. By lungs
D. By tracheal system

✅ **Correct Answer: D. By tracheal system**

---

# Core Concept

Most insects respire using a **tracheal system**, which is a network of **air-filled tubes that directly supply oxygen to body tissues**.

Unlike humans, insects **do not use blood to transport oxygen**.

Instead, air moves directly through tubes called **tracheae**.

---

# Structure of the Tracheal System

The respiratory system of insects consists of three main components:

1. **Spiracles**

• Small openings present on the **sides of the insect body**.

2. **Tracheae**

• Air tubes that transport **oxygen throughout the body**.

3. **Tracheoles**

• Extremely fine branches that deliver **oxygen directly to individual cells**.

Air pathway:

[
\text{Spiracles} \rightarrow \text{Tracheae} \rightarrow \text{Tracheoles} \rightarrow \text{Cells}
]

Examples of insects using this system include:

• Grasshopper
• Cockroach
• Ant
• Honeybee

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Through skin ❌ (Incorrect)

Respiration through skin is called **cutaneous respiration**.

Organisms that use this method include:

• Earthworms
• Frogs (partially)

Their skin must remain **moist for gas exchange**.

Insects possess a **hard exoskeleton**, which prevents respiration through skin.

---

## B. Through gills ❌ (Incorrect)

**Gills** are respiratory organs used by **aquatic animals**.

Examples include:

• Fish
• Tadpoles
• Some aquatic insect larvae

Gills extract oxygen from **water rather than air**.

Most adult insects live on land and **do not possess gills**.

---

## C. By lungs ❌ (Incorrect)

**Lungs** are respiratory organs present in **vertebrates**, such as:

• Humans
• Birds
• Reptiles
• Mammals

Insects **do not possess lungs**.

Their relatively small body size allows **direct oxygen delivery through the tracheal system**.

---

## D. By tracheal system ✅ (Correct)

The **tracheal system** is the characteristic respiratory mechanism of insects.

Key features:

• Air enters through **spiracles**.
• Oxygen travels through **tracheal tubes**.
• Oxygen reaches tissues **directly without involvement of blood**.

Therefore insects respire through a **tracheal system**.

---

# Quick Comparison of Respiration in Animals

| Organism | Respiratory Organ |
|---|---|
| Earthworm | Skin |
| Fish | Gills |
| Humans | Lungs |
| Insects | Tracheal system |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

If a question mentions **"respiration in insects"**, the correct answer is almost always:

👉 **Tracheal system**.

---

If required, you can also study **important respiration-related MCQs frequently asked in DRDO, SSC, and banking examinations**, which have a **high probability of appearing in Tier-1 exams**.

questions.text: Fishes survive under the frozen lake, because
options: Fishes are in hibernation under it
Water does not freeze near bottom
Ice is the conductor of heat
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Water does not freeze near bottom (C)**

Ice floats; bottom water stays at 4°C (maximum density). DRDO naval research uses this for cold-water diving physiology and submarine operations.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Others factually incorrect.

**Important Exam Concept:** Anomalous water expansion & aquatic survival (naval ops).

questions.text: After bringing out of water fishes die because
options: They get excess quantity of oxygen
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: They cannot breathe (C)**

Fishes use gills for dissolved oxygen; air has no dissolved O₂ for them. DRDO relevance: diver training and naval rescue physiology.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Others incorrect or secondary.

**Important Exam Concept:** Aquatic respiration & naval technology.

questions.text: Fish generally breathe through
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Gills (C)**

Counter-current exchange extracts dissolved O₂. DRDO: gill-mimicry in underwater breathing apparatus and sonar studies.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Others not respiratory organs.

**Important Exam Concept:** Aquatic respiration & naval technology.

questions.text: Redness in apple is due to
questions.solution:
This question comes from **plant pigments**, a small but frequently tested topic in the Biology section of **DRDO CEPTAM-11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Redness in apple is due to**

A. Anthocyanin
B. Lycopene
C. Carotene
D. Xanthophyll

✅ **Correct Answer: A. Anthocyanin**

---

# Core Concept

The **red colour in apples** is caused by **anthocyanin pigments**.

**Anthocyanins** are **water-soluble pigments** present in plant cell vacuoles and belong to the **flavonoid group**.

They produce colours such as:

• **Red**
• **Purple**
• **Blue**

Examples of plants containing anthocyanins include:

• Apple skin
• Red cabbage
• Grapes
• Berries
• Rose petals

These pigments also function as **antioxidants**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Anthocyanin ✔ (Correct)

**Anthocyanins** are responsible for **red, purple, and blue colours in plants**.

Examples:

| Plant | Colour Pigment |
|---|---|
| Apple | Anthocyanin |
| Grapes | Anthocyanin |
| Red cabbage | Anthocyanin |

Thus the **red colour of apple peel** is produced by **anthocyanin pigments**.

---

## B. Lycopene ✘ (Incorrect)

**Lycopene** is a **red carotenoid pigment**.

It produces the red colour of:

• **Tomato**
• **Watermelon**
• **Red guava**

However, **apple redness is not due to lycopene**.

---

## C. Carotene ✘ (Incorrect)

**Carotene** pigments produce **orange colour**.

Examples:

• Carrot
• Pumpkin
• Sweet potato

The word **carotene** originates from **carrot**.

Therefore it **does not cause the red colour of apples**.

---

## D. Xanthophyll ✘ (Incorrect)

**Xanthophyll** pigments produce **yellow colour**.

Examples:

• Maize (corn)
• Banana
• Egg yolk
• Yellow leaves

Thus it is **not responsible for the red colour of apples**.

---

# Important Pigments to Remember for Exams

| Pigment | Colour | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Chlorophyll | Green | Leaves |
| Carotene | Orange | Carrot |
| Xanthophyll | Yellow | Maize |
| Lycopene | Red | Tomato |
| Anthocyanin | Red / Purple / Blue | Apple, Grapes |

---

# 10-Second Memory Trick

Remember these quick associations:

• **Carrot → Carotene (orange)**
• **Tomato → Lycopene (red)**
• **Apple / Grapes → Anthocyanin (red or purple)**
• **Maize → Xanthophyll (yellow)**

---

If needed, you can also study **important plant pigment tables frequently asked in DRDO, SSC, and banking examinations**, which often appear in **Tier-1 general science questions**.

questions.text: Red colour in tomato is due to
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Lycopene (B)**

Carotenoid antioxidant giving red colour. DRDO: antioxidant-rich supplements for soldiers.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Capsaicin (A): Chillies. Xanthophyll (C): Yellow pigments.

**Important Exam Concept:** Carotenoids & health in defence rations.

questions.text: The yellow colour of papaya is due to
questions.solution:
This question is from **plant pigments and plant products**, a small but commonly tested topic in the Biology section of **DRDO CEPTAM-11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**The yellow colour of papaya is due to**

A. Papain
B. Lycopene
C. Caricaxanthin
D. Carotene

✅ **Correct Answer: C. Caricaxanthin**

---

# Core Concept

The **yellow colour of papaya fruit** is mainly due to the pigment **caricaxanthin**.

**Caricaxanthin** is a **carotenoid pigment** present in papaya pulp that gives it a **yellow to orange colour**.

It belongs to the **xanthophyll group of carotenoids**, which are pigments responsible for yellow colours in many plants.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Papain ❌ (Incorrect)

**Papain** is **not a pigment**.

It is a **proteolytic enzyme** found in **papaya latex (milky sap)**.

Functions:

• Breaks down proteins
• Used in **meat tenderizers**
• Used in **digestive medicines**
• Used in **food processing industries**

Thus papain is **an enzyme**, not responsible for colour.

---

## B. Lycopene ❌ (Incorrect)

**Lycopene** is a **red carotenoid pigment**.

Examples of lycopene-rich foods:

• Tomato
• Watermelon
• Red guava

Since papaya pulp is mainly **yellow/orange**, lycopene is **not the primary pigment responsible**.

---

## C. Caricaxanthin ✔ (Correct)

**Caricaxanthin** is the **main pigment responsible for the yellow colour of papaya**.

Characteristics:

• A **carotenoid pigment**
• Produces **yellow coloration**
• Found specifically in **papaya fruit**

Therefore this option is **correct**.

---

## D. Carotene ❌ (Incorrect)

**Carotene** pigments usually produce **orange colour**.

Examples:

• Carrot
• Pumpkin
• Sweet potato

Although papaya contains some carotenoids, the **specific pigment responsible for its yellow colour is caricaxanthin**, not carotene.

---

# Important Pigments to Remember for Exams

| Fruit / Plant | Pigment |
|---|---|
| Tomato | Lycopene |
| Carrot | Carotene |
| Apple (red) | Anthocyanin |
| Papaya | Caricaxanthin |
| Turmeric | Curcumin |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Memorize these quick associations:

• **Tomato → Lycopene**
• **Carrot → Carotene**
• **Apple → Anthocyanin**
• **Papaya → Caricaxanthin**

---

If required, you can also study **important plant pigments and plant products (such as papain, quinine, morphine, curcumin)** that frequently appear in **DRDO, SSC, and banking competitive examinations**.

questions.text: Opium is obtained from which part of the poppy plant?
questions.solution:
This question comes from **plant products / economic botany**, which appears occasionally in the Biology section of **DRDO CEPTAM-11 Tier-1**.

---

# Question

**Opium is obtained from which part of the poppy plant?**

A. Flower
B. Leaves
C. Unripe fruits
D. Roots

✅ **Correct Answer: C. Unripe fruits**

---

# Core Concept

Opium is obtained from the **latex of the unripe capsule (fruit)** of the plant **Papaver somniferum**.

Process:

1. The **unripe fruit capsule** of the poppy plant is **cut or scratched**.
2. A **milky latex** oozes out from the capsule.
3. This latex **dries and thickens** on exposure to air.
4. The dried latex is collected as **opium**.

This latex contains several **alkaloids**, including:

• Morphine
• Codeine
• Thebaine
• Papaverine

These compounds are widely used in **pain-relieving medicines and pharmaceuticals**.

---

# Explanation of Each Option

## A. Flower ❌ (Incorrect)

The **flower of the poppy plant** mainly functions in **reproduction and seed formation**.

It **does not produce the latex** used to obtain opium.

Therefore, flowers are **not the source of opium**.

---

## B. Leaves ❌ (Incorrect)

Leaves primarily perform functions such as:

• **Photosynthesis**
• **Gas exchange**
• **Transpiration**

They **do not contain the latex required for opium extraction**, making this option incorrect.

---

## C. Unripe fruits ✔ (Correct)

The **unripe capsule (fruit)** of the poppy plant contains **latex rich in alkaloids**.

When the capsule is **incised**, the latex oozes out and **dries to form opium**.

Thus opium is obtained from the **unripe fruits of the poppy plant**.

---

## D. Roots ❌ (Incorrect)

Roots mainly perform:

• **Absorption of water and minerals from soil**
• **Anchorage of the plant**

They **do not produce the latex used to prepare opium**, therefore this option is incorrect.

---

# Quick Exam Facts (High Probability)

| Plant Product | Source |
|---|---|
| Opium | Unripe capsule of poppy |
| Papain | Papaya latex |
| Quinine | Cinchona bark |
| Curcumin | Turmeric rhizome |
| Morphine | Alkaloid from opium |

---

# 10-Second Exam Trick

Remember the association:

**Poppy capsule → Opium**

or

**Opium = latex of unripe poppy fruit**.

---

You can also study **important plant products and their sources (such as quinine, morphine, caffeine, nicotine, rubber, etc.)**, which are **frequently asked in DRDO, SSC, and banking competitive examinations**. | Plant Product | Source Plant | Part Used |
|---|---|---|
| Opium | Papaver somniferum | Unripe fruit capsule |
| Morphine | Papaver somniferum | Opium latex |
| Codeine | Papaver somniferum | Opium latex |
| Quinine | Cinchona officinalis | Bark |
| Caffeine | Coffea arabica | Seeds |
| Theine (same as caffeine) | Camellia sinensis | Leaves |
| Nicotine | Nicotiana tabacum | Leaves |
| Papain | Carica papaya | Latex of fruit |
| Curcumin | Curcuma longa | Rhizome |
| Menthol | Mentha piperita | Leaves |
| Rubber | Hevea brasiliensis | Latex |
| Indigo dye | Indigofera tinctoria | Leaves |
| Reserpine | Rauvolfia serpentina | Roots |

questions.text: Which of the following groups of living beings belong to the same species?
options: Chinese, American, Indians and Black Africans
questions.solution:

All are Homo sapiens. DRDO human factors research: uniform physiological/ergonomic standards for diverse troops.

**Option-wise Analysis:**

**Important Exam Concept:** Biological species concept & human evolution.

questions.text: Yeast is an example of
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Fungus (B)**

Unicellular ascomycete fungus. DRDO: fermentation for biofuel, food preservation and ration production.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Others wrong kingdom.

**Important Exam Concept:** Fungi classification & industrial microbiology.

questions.text: Yeast and mushrooms are
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Fungi (C)**

Heterotrophic, chitinous cell wall. DRDO: mushroom cultivation for protein-rich soldier diet and mycorrhizal biofertilizers.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Others wrong group.

**Important Exam Concept:** Kingdom Fungi & applied mycology.

questions.text: What is a mushroom?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Fungi (A)**

Basidiomycete fruiting body. DRDO: protein source for high-altitude rations.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Others incorrect.

**Important Exam Concept:** Edible fungi & nutritional security.
